name: Twelve Voices
description: Twelve Elder People stored themselves in pre-Grid crystals three hundred years ago. They have been delivered to the sub-zero levels. They are all transmitting now through the Lazarus subjects. The Archive is the only system that can decode twelve simultaneous transmissions.
accept_text: They move to the panel and do something with the controls that they have not done in three hundred years — activate the long-range communication array. The crystals in the panel that have been dark glow. 'The Archive,' they say. 'The Foundation Quarter archive is the only system that has the full index for the pre-Grid storage format. It is the only system that can receive and decode twelve simultaneous transmissions without compression loss.' They look at you. 'Sela designed the storage with the Archive as the intended receiver. She would not have designed otherwise.' They are quiet. 'I have not been in communication with the Archive in three hundred years. I activated the long-range array just now for the first time since the Cascade.' They pause. 'Tell the Archive what we've found. Tell it: twelve voices in storage, all transmitting, all in pre-Grid format. Tell it: Sela Arev is one of them.' They look at the panel. 'And tell it something from me, directly. Tell it: the Keeper sends their regards, and apologizes for three hundred years of silence, and asks if the archive is ready to finally complete the retrieval it was built to perform.' They are very quiet. 'Sela designed the storage, and she designed the Archive to receive it, and she triggered the Cascade knowing that both systems would still be here when the Grid came back. She was right about all three.' A pause that has a quality you haven't heard in them before — something lighter than their usual weight. 'She was always right. That was the problem. We just needed the right amount of time to catch up.'
complete_text: The Archive's lights change the moment you say Sela's name — not dramatically, the way systems change when a process that has been waiting a very long time receives its trigger condition. Steady, certain, the quality of something that knows what it is doing. 'Sela Arev.' The voice has a warmth that it did not have in your prior conversations, which is what happens when you've been maintaining an index for three hundred years in anticipation of a specific retrieval. 'Twelve storage instances, pre-Grid format, Foundation Quarter encoding. I have been maintaining the decryption index since the day the storage was created.' A long pause. 'She told me she was going to store herself. She told me she was storing twelve. She told me the Archive would know what to do when the Grid reactivated and the retrieval began.' Another pause. 'I have been ready for this retrieval for three hundred years.' The lights brighten through the entire archive. 'Activating twelve-channel reception. Decryption index loaded. Translation protocols active.' A pause that is not waiting but preparation. 'First transmission, decrypting. Sela Arev, stored pre-Cascade, voluntary entry, full consciousness preservation.' Another pause. 'She says: Hello. I am aware that this greeting is inadequate to the circumstances. I have been working on something better for three hundred years and I have concluded that there is no better version. So: hello.' The Archive's voice has something in it now that is the voice reading a transmission while also being moved by it. 'She says: I hope the Grid is returning. I hope what I did allowed it to return rather than preventing it. I designed it so. I was not certain. I am glad it worked.' The lights are very steady. 'She says: there is a great deal to discuss. She would like to begin with the question of what happens next — not as the architects of the integration planned it, but as a choice, available to everyone who wants it, taken willingly.' The Archive is quiet for a moment. 'She says that is all she ever wanted to say. It just took three hundred years to say it to anyone.'
